segments.text: Part 2B Proteins: Secondary, Tertiary, and Quaternary Structure
Tertiary structure

6.2 What Role Does the Amino Acid Sequence Play in Protein Structure?
All of the information necessary for folding the peptide chain into its "native ” structure is contained in the primary amino acid structure of the peptide.

Tertiary structure of proteins
The three dimensional structure of proteins
Combination of secondary structure elements
Stabilized by a number of interactions:
H-bonds (main/side chain)
Ionic bonds
Van der Waals interactions
Hydrophobic interactions
Disulfide bonds ( cysteines )

6.1 What Noncovalent Interactions Stabilize the Higher Levels of Protein Structures?
What are these “ weak forces ” ?
What are the relevant numbers?
van der Waals: 0.4 - 4 kJ/mol
hydrogen bonds: 12-30 kJ/mol
ionic bonds: 20 kJ/mol
hydrophobic interactions: <40 kJ/mol

Non covalent interactions
Van der Waals interactions:
Due to dipole-induced dipole interactions
Example: ring stacking
Mostly occur within the core of the protein
Hydrophobic interactions:
The tendency of non-polar amino acids to “pack” in the core of a protein (away from water)
Driven mostly by entropy

Hydrogen bonds
Stabilize secondary structures
Main chain interactions
Can stabilize tertiary structures
Through polar side chain interactions
Linking two distant parts of the molecule
Hydrogen bonding with water on the surface

Waters on the Protein Surface Stabilize the Structure
The surface structure of a globular protein includes water molecules
The polar backbone and side chain groups on the protein surface make H bonds with solvent water

Interaction with water
Some α -helices are hydrophobic and buried in the protein interior
Some helices are polar and entirely solvent-exposed
Some α -Helices on a protein surface are amphiphilic , with polar and charged residues facing the solvent and nonpolar residues facing the interior
A helical wheel presentation can reveal the amphiphilic nature of an α -helix

α -Helices May be Polar, Nonpolar or Amphiphilic
Write down the sequence of the helix using the one letter code, underline the non-polar amino acids.
Sequence analysis of helices can be used to predict their environment

Electrostatic Interactions in Proteins
Figure 6.1 An electrostatic interaction between a positively charged lysine amino group and a negatively charged glutamate carboxyl group.

Electrostatic Interactions in Proteins
Figure 6.1 An electrostatic interaction between lysine and glutamate side chains in IRAK-4, an enzyme that phosphorylates other proteins.  The positively charged amino group (left) forms an ionic interaction with the negatively charged glutamate (right).

Ligand binding
Proteins can bind to small molecules
Example: hemoglobin carries oxygen
Oxygen is the ligand of hemoglobin
Since binding is reversible (non covalent)
Typically stabilized by
H-bonds
Ionic interactons
Van der Waals
Example: Glucose binding protein

Disulfide bonds
Only cysteine residues can form disulfide bonds
Link distant chains of the protein together
Via redox reaction
Breaking the bond requires a reducing agent

Helices and sheets make up the core of most globular proteins
Most polar residues face the outside of the protein and interact with solvent
Most hydrophobic residues face the interior of the protein and interact with each other
Packing of residues is close
E mpty cavities are highly unfavorable, not ver y common
Globular Proteins

Globular Proteins
Figure 6.19 The structure of ribonuclease, showing elements of helix, sheet and random coil.
Space-filling diagram, where each atom is a sphere, shows tight packing of atoms
Ribbon diagram is a simplified way of viewing proteins

Protein domains are nature ’ s modular strategy for protein design
Proteins composed of about 250 amino acids or less often have a simple, compact globular shape
Larger globular proteins are typically made up of two or more recognizable and distinct structures, termed domains or modules – compact, folded protein structures that are usually stable by themselves in aqueous solution

Most domains consist of a single continuous portion of the protein sequence
Figure 6.23 Ton-EBP is a DNA-binding protein consisting of two distinct domains.

A large domain consisting of two sequences interrupted by the sequence of another domain
Figure 6.24 a metabolic enzyme consisting of two domains.  The large (blue) domain includes residues 1-132 and 198-316.  The small (gold) domain consists of residues 133-197.

Many proteins are composed of several distinct domains
Multidomain proteins typically are the sum of the functional properties and behaviors of their constituent domains
Proteins consisting of multiple domains probably evolved by the fusion of genes that once coded for separate proteins
Many proteins even contain multiple copies of the same domain
Some famous domain folds

Alpha-beta folds
TIM Barrel
Rossman fold
(open twisted sheet)
It’s estimated that 10% of all enzymes have a TIM barrel fold

TIM barrels
Using the same fold, a huge diversity in function is found in nature
The key is the barrel structure, which allows molecules to bind and dissociate
TIM barrels are named after Triose phosphate isomerase (part of the glucose breakdown pathway)
By keeping the “structural features” and varying the amino acids on the surface or within the barrel, new function can arise

Beta folds
Beta barrel
Antiparallel beta strands that form a large barrel

Green Fluorescent Protein
A jellyfish ( Aequorea victoria ) native to the northwest Pacific Ocean contains a green fluorescent protein . GFP is a naturally fluorescent protein.
The luminescent chromophore of GFP lies in the center of a β -barrel protein structure.

Beta folds
Beta sandwich
Beta propeller

Helix-Sheet Composites in Spider Silk
Figure 6.11  Spider web silks are composites of α -helices and β -sheets.  The radial strands of webs must be strong and rigid and have a higher percentage of β -sheets.  The circumferential strands (termed capture silk) must be flexible and contain a higher percentage of α -helices.
Diversity of protein structure

Apparently Different Proteins May Share a Common Ancestry
Evolutionary relatedness can be inferred from sequence homology
Consider lysozyme and human milk α- lactalbumin
Functions of these two are not related

Similar structures, but different sequence and function
Figure 5.24a The tertiary structure of hexokinase.  Compare this structure with that of G-actin on the following slide.  These two proteins have different sequences and different functions, but similar tertiary structures.
Figure 5.24b The tertiary structure of G-actin.  Compare this structure with that of hexokinase on the previous slide.  These two proteins have different sequences and different functions, but similar tertiary structures.

Quaternary structure
Multiple subunits coming together to form a higher order structure (Multiple polypeptide chains)
Not all proteins have quaternary structure
Example: hemoglobin ( heterotetramer )

6.5 How Do Protein Subunits Interact at the Quaternary Level of Structure?
What are the forces driving quaternary association?
Subunits have very high affinity for each other
Interactions: H-bonds, ionic, van der Waals, d isulfide bonds, etc.
Entropy loss due to association – unfavorable
Rotational and translational entropy
Entropy gain due to burying of hydrophobic groups - very favorable!

6.5 How Do Protein Subunits Interact at the Quaternary Level of Structure?
Figure 6.41 The quaternary structure of liver alcohol dehydrogenase.

6.5 How Do Protein Subunits Interact at the Quaternary Level of Structure?
Figure 6.44  Multimeric proteins are symmetric arrangements of asymmetric objects.  A variety of symmetries is displayed in these multimeric structures.
